Building Pagination From Scratch in JavaScript (with the Fetch API)

When a site has a lot of data, you end up needing pagination. Sometimes the backend handles it and just sends you one page at a time — that's the easy case, you mostly just configure things on the database side and don't have to worry. But if the backend dumps all the data to the frontend in one go, then you have to build the pagination logic yourself. So I wrote this post to drill the pagination logic into my own head.
First, it helps to nail down the big-picture requirements.
Generally there are two key numbers you start from, and everything else extends out from there:
How many items to show per page

We'll build this around the two key numbers above, but first we have to fetch the data (I'm using ES6 Fetch here, not XHR).
No surprise — you should see the returned response info in the console. But our actual data lives inside a ReadableStream, so we need to process it one more time.

Now for the harder part. So far we've only covered pulling data remotely — next comes actually writing the pagination logic.
Like I mentioned, pagination basically comes down to these two things:
, pass in the data we just pulled, and start sketching out a basic skeleton based on those two numbers.
// how many items to show on screen — default to 5 per page
Next we need somewhere for the page numbers — those 1 2 3 4 buttons on the pager.
So we have to work out the total number of pages (which is not the same as the total number of items). The formula is: total items / items per page.
// page button count = total items / items per page
// heads up: this can leave a remainder, so we round up.
Next we build the current page.
We want to avoid the current page being larger than the total number of pages. Say the total is 3 pages — there's no way the current page should be 4 or 5. So we add a check to prevent that.
// when the "current page" is bigger than the "total pages", clamp it to "total pages"

When you switch pages, the data has to update accordingly instead of staying stuck on the same page. So if we show 5 items per page, page one gives us items 1 through 5, and on page two it gives us items 6 through 10.

Last thing — we go back and tweak one spot: the
function needs to take in a value, so we add another parameter.

I honestly didn't expect pagination logic to be this fiddly — I got stuck at one point and was hunting around for answers to reference.
I worked through it using an approach taught in some online courses, but writing it out myself once and documenting it really helped it stick.
Of course there's room to make this pagination logic more complete, but I didn't bother — it's just a small exercise, I purely wanted to wrap my head around the pagination logic.

When a site has a lot of data, you need pagination. Sometimes the backend handles it and just hands you a page at a time — easy. But what if the backend dumps everything to the frontend at once? Then you've got to build the pagination logic yourself. This post walks through doing exactly that: pulling data with the Fetch API and writing the pagination logic by hand.
